stem: A hospital uses an HbA1c test to screen for Type 2 diabetes. The test has a sensitivity of 98% and a specificity of 82%. The prevalence of Type 2 diabetes in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.5% and 2.7%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has Type 2 diabetes? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(Type 2 diabetes | positive test) ranges from 0.0766 to 0.131 as prevalence varies from 1.5% to 2.7%. Point estimate at 2.1% prevalence: 0.105.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.18.
The confidence interval [0.0766, 0.131] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (98%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
